:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Parole-Eligible Prison Population

In 2020, there were 1,454 people who were eligible for parole but remained incarcerated for a new crime. This group made up 4% of the prison population.



``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Release Eligibility Determined?

An inmate with an in- determinate sentence may be paroled at “any time” after the expiration of their minimum term of incarceration. The minimum term of incarceration can be reduced through time credits. Determinate sentences involve a fixed length of prison time with no eligibility for parole release; however time credits can be applied to the sentence and result in early release when 6/7 of the full sentence is complete. This type of early release is called “conditional release,” and is supervised in the same manner as parole. Inmates may be conditionally released by DOCCS, not the Board of Parole. Determinate sentences also normally require a period of “post-release supervision” which is an additional part of the sentence. Conditional release is also available to those serving indeterminate sentences after they have completed 2/3 of their minimum sentence. Presumptive release exists for certain non-violent offenders who have received a certificate of earned eligibility from the State Commissioner of Corrections and Community Supervision. This allows inmates to return to the community under supervision after 5/6 of their prison term is finished, at the discretion of the Board.


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

People who are Eligible for Parole

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Race ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, Black, non-Hispanic people constituted the most number of people eligible for parole but still in prison for a new crime, accounting for 43% (631 people) of the parole-eligible prison population.


``` ::: ::: ### Gender ### Age ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Most Serious Sentenced Offense

In 2020, there were 719 people who were parole eligible but still in prison for non-violent offenses, accounting for 49% of the parole-eligible prison population in prison for new crimes.

``` ::: :::

## Disparities
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Descriptive Statistics

``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
Race, ethincity, age, and gender

``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Relative Rate Index

The relative rate index (RRI) provides a measure of disparity in outcomes for different racial and ethnic groups by comparing the rates at which individuals of different races and ethnic groups experience incarceration. The rate for each specific racial and ethnic group is computed by dividing the population of interest within that group by the total state population for that particular group.

To calculate the RRI, the rate of each outcome of interest for individuals who are Black non-Hispanic and Hispanic any race, is divided by the rate at which the outcome occurs for individuals who are White. A RRI of 1 indicates no disparity. RRIs greater than 1 indicate the outcome occurs more frequently for individuals who are Black or Hispanic, and an RRI less than 1 indicates an outcome occurs less frequently than for individuals who are White.
